title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read an article from which six sentences have been removed. Choose from the sentences A–G the one which fits each gap (37–42). There is one extra sentence which you do not need to use.

content.title: Food Trucks: The Revolution on Wheels
content.text: On a drizzly Tuesday lunchtime in downtown Portland, a queue of fifty people snakes along the pavement. They are not waiting for a famous restaurant. They are waiting for a small, brightly painted truck selling Korean-Mexican fusion tacos. The food truck phenomenon has fundamentally reshaped urban eating habits over the past fifteen years. (37) [37] What began as a way for entrepreneurs to enter the restaurant industry cheaply has evolved into a respected culinary movement. [/37] || For chefs, the appeal is obvious. Opening a traditional restaurant requires enormous capital investment, a long lease, and a brigade of staff. A food truck demands far less of each. (38) [38] The flexibility to move locations, test menus and build a following on social media gives truck operators an agility that bricks-and-mortar restaurants simply cannot match. [/38] Failure is still possible, but the financial stakes are considerably lower. || The food itself has risen dramatically in quality. Early street food carts were associated with fast, cheap meals — hot dogs, pretzels, simple sandwiches. Today, food trucks offer everything from wood-fired pizza to elaborate desserts using liquid nitrogen. (39) [39] Some trucks have even earned recognition in prestigious food guides, a development that would have seemed unimaginable a generation ago. [/39] || Cities have had to adapt their regulations to manage the trend. In some places, permits are tightly controlled and competition fierce; in others, designated 'food truck parks' have become popular social venues in their own right. (40) [40] These clusters of trucks, often accompanied by outdoor seating and live music, have become a new kind of urban gathering place. [/40] || Not everyone welcomes the trucks unreservedly. Fixed-location restaurateurs sometimes complain that trucks enjoy an unfair advantage by avoiding the overheads of permanent premises. (41) [41] The debate reflects wider tensions about how cities should balance innovation with the protection of established businesses. [/41] || Despite the controversies, the food truck industry continues to grow. (42) [42] For food lovers, that means more variety, more creativity and more reasons to brave a queue on a drizzly lunchtime. [/42]

paragraphs.A: Chefs who might never have opened their own restaurant now have a viable path into the business.
paragraphs.B: The standard of cooking has kept pace with — and in some cases surpassed — that of conventional restaurants.
paragraphs.C: City authorities in many countries are still developing frameworks to regulate this relatively new sector.
paragraphs.D: These parks have transformed neglected urban spaces into vibrant community hubs.
paragraphs.E: This tension between tradition and disruption is not unique to the food industry.
paragraphs.F: The low overhead model also enables operators to experiment with bold, unusual flavour combinations.
paragraphs.G: New operators enter the market every year, drawn by relatively low start-up costs and the promise of creative freedom.

questions.explanation: A explains that chefs who would never have opened their own restaurant now have a way in, supporting the idea that it started as a cheap way to enter the industry.
F mentions the low-cost model that allows experimentation, connecting with the flexibility to test menus described before the gap.
B asserts that the quality of cooking has equalled or surpassed that of conventional restaurants, reinforcing the rise in quality described in the paragraph.
D describes the parks as vibrant community centres, detailing the concept of 'food truck parks' mentioned just before.
E notes that the tension between tradition and innovation is not exclusive to the food sector, generalising the debate about unfair advantages.
G explains that every year new operators are attracted by the low costs and creative freedom, supporting the idea of continued growth.
